content.text: Post-Incident Customer Follow-Up

The moment a status page changes to 'Resolved' is not the end of customer communication—it is the beginning of the follow-up phase. Customers who experienced disruption need confirmation that the issue is fully resolved, that their data and requests are intact, and that the team is taking steps to prevent recurrence. Teams that close the incident internally and go silent after resolution lose the opportunity to turn a negative experience into a demonstration of operational maturity.
Core Concept

Post-incident customer follow-up has three components: the resolution communication itself, the postmortem that explains what happened and what is being fixed, and the SLA credit or contractual obligation fulfillment. Each must be executed on a defined timeline after resolution. Delaying or skipping any of these components signals to customers that the team is not fully accountable for its reliability commitments.
Architecture: Follow-Up Timeline

attrs.alt: Post-incident customer follow-up timeline from T+0 resolution to T+30 days action item close-out
attrs.title: Post-Incident Customer Follow-Up Timeline

content.text: Immediate Follow-Up (T+0 to T+1 hour)
Final Status Page Update (T+5 min)

The resolved status page entry must include: exact resolution time, affected period, brief impact description, workaround note if any actions are needed by users, and a postmortem commitment with expected publication date.
This is not the postmortem itself—it is a commitment to publish one. The full resolution template:

Title: {Service} Incident — Resolved
Status: Resolved

{Service} returned to normal operation at {resolution_time} UTC.

Affected period: {start_time}–{resolution_time} UTC ({duration} minutes)

Impact: {impact_description_in_user_terms}

{action_note}

We will publish a full incident report including root cause analysis
and preventive actions at:
{status_page_url}/incidents/{incident_id}
Target publication: {postmortem_deadline}

We apologize for the disruption.

--- action_note options ---
'Requests that failed during this period should be retried — data was not affected.'
'No action is required; affected requests were automatically retried by our system.'
'Please check your {specific_feature} configuration if you observe unexpected state.'
Support Ticket Resolution (T+1 hour)

Every support ticket opened during the incident must be closed within one hour of resolution with a message linking to the status page resolved entry. Customers who opened tickets deserve a personal notification, not just a public status page update they may not be watching.
Short-Term Follow-Up (T+24 hours)
Enterprise SLA Impact Report

Enterprise customers under SLA contracts require a direct notification of the SLA impact within 24 hours of resolution—proactively, before the customer requests it. This report does not need to contain the full root cause analysis (that comes in the postmortem), but must include the factual impact data needed for SLA credit calculation.
Medium-Term Follow-Up (T+5 Business Days)
Customer-Facing Postmortem

The customer-facing postmortem is a curated, non-technical summary of the internal postmortem. It must be factually accurate but should not include internal system names, team-level blame, speculative future risks, or confidential architecture details.
A customer-facing postmortem has five sections:
Summary.
What happened, when, how long, and how many users were affected. One paragraph.
Impact.
Specific, measurable impact description. Error rates, latency numbers, percentage of users affected, geographic scope.
Root Cause.
Customer-understandable explanation of why the incident happened. No internal jargon, no blame, no speculation.
Resolution.
What was done to restore service and how long it took.
Preventive Actions.

Concrete steps being taken to prevent recurrence, with target timelines. Vague promises ('improve our processes') are less credible than specific actions ('deploy circuit breaker on the embedding pipeline by Q3 2026').

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import date
from typing import Optional

@dataclass
class PostmortemAction:
    description: str          # Customer-visible description of preventive action
    target_date: Optional[date] = None
    status: str = 'planned'   # planned | in-progress | complete

    def to_customer_text(self) -> str:
        date_str = f' (target: {self.target_date.strftime("%B %Y")})' if self.target_date else ''
        status_str = ' [complete]' if self.status == 'complete' else ''
        return f'- {self.description}{date_str}{status_str}'

@dataclass
class CustomerPostmortem:
    incident_id: str
    service: str
    start_time: str             # ISO format string
    resolution_time: str
    duration_minutes: int
    users_affected_pct: float
    root_cause_summary: str     # Plain language, no internals
    resolution_summary: str
    actions: list[PostmortemAction] = field(default_factory=list)
    additional_context: Optional[str] = None

    def render_markdown(self) -> str:
        actions_text = '\n'.join(a.to_customer_text() for a in self.actions)
        context_section = (
            f'\n## Additional Context\n{self.additional_context}'
            if self.additional_context else ''
        )
        return f"""# Incident Report: {self.incident_id}

## Summary
On {self.start_time}, {self.service} experienced a service disruption
lasting {self.duration_minutes} minutes. Approximately {self.users_affected_pct:.0f}%
of users were affected.

## Root Cause
{self.root_cause_summary}

## Resolution
{self.resolution_summary} The incident was resolved at {self.resolution_time}.

## Preventive Actions
We are implementing the following steps to prevent recurrence:
{actions_text}{context_section}

---
We take reliability seriously and appreciate your patience.
For questions, contact support@company.com
"""
Long-Term Follow-Up (T+30 Days)

Thirty days after resolution, conduct an action item close-out check. Every preventive action committed to in the postmortem should have a current status. Update the public postmortem page if actions are complete or if timelines have changed.
Updating a published postmortem with action completion status is rare but builds significant trust. It demonstrates that the team follows through on commitments—not just that it makes them.
GenAI-Specific Follow-Up Considerations
Affected request replay.

For GenAI incidents where requests failed and users may need their data regenerated, provide a mechanism or offer support for replaying affected requests. API customers in particular may have lost work products.
Output quality validation confirmation.

For output quality degradation incidents, the follow-up must confirm that quality has been restored and briefly describe how it was validated (e.g., 'We ran our standard evaluation suite and confirmed response quality returned to baseline').
Safety incident sensitivity.

If the incident involved harmful AI outputs, the follow-up requires special care. The postmortem must acknowledge the harm to affected users without attributing fault to them, describe what safeguards failed and what is replacing them, and provide a contact for users who want to report lasting impact.
Measuring Follow-Up Quality
Post-incident follow-up is an operational discipline with measurable outcomes. Track these metrics across incidents to identify patterns and improve follow-up consistency:
Metric
Target (SEV-1)
Measurement Method
Time to final status update
< 5 min after resolution
Status page update timestamp vs. resolution timestamp
Support ticket close rate
100% within 1 hour
Ticket system close timestamp vs. resolution timestamp
Postmortem publication rate
100% within 5 business days
Status page incident log checked at 5-day mark
Enterprise SLA report delivery
100% within 24 hours
Email tracking vs. resolution timestamp
Postmortem action completion rate
> 80% within committed timeline
30-day action close-out review
Follow-Up Automation and Tracking

Manual tracking of follow-up tasks across multiple incidents fails. A follow-up tracker—either built into the incident management platform or as a lightweight service—ensures nothing falls through the cracks.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import datetime, timedelta, timezone
from enum import Enum
from typing import Optional

class FollowUpTask(Enum):
    FINAL_STATUS_UPDATE = 'final_status_update'
    SUPPORT_TICKET_CLOSE = 'support_ticket_close'
    ENTERPRISE_SLA_REPORT = 'enterprise_sla_report'
    POSTMORTEM_PUBLISH = 'postmortem_publish'
    ACTION_ITEM_CLOSEOUT = 'action_item_closeout'

@dataclass
class IncidentFollowUpPlan:
    incident_id: str
    severity: int
    resolution_time: datetime
    has_enterprise_customers: bool = False
    postmortem_days: int = 5

    def task_deadlines(self) -> dict[FollowUpTask, datetime]:
        t = self.resolution_time
        return {
            FollowUpTask.FINAL_STATUS_UPDATE:
                t + timedelta(minutes=5),
            FollowUpTask.SUPPORT_TICKET_CLOSE:
                t + timedelta(hours=1),
            FollowUpTask.ENTERPRISE_SLA_REPORT:
                t + timedelta(hours=24) if self.has_enterprise_customers else None,
            FollowUpTask.POSTMORTEM_PUBLISH:
                t + timedelta(days=self.postmortem_days),
            FollowUpTask.ACTION_ITEM_CLOSEOUT:
                t + timedelta(days=30),
        }

    def schedule_reminders(self, notify_fn) -> None:
        """Schedule Slack reminders for each follow-up task."""
        deadlines = self.task_deadlines()
        for task, deadline in deadlines.items():
            if deadline is None:
                continue
            now = datetime.now(timezone.utc)
            delay_seconds = max(0, (deadline - now).total_seconds() - 300)  # 5 min early
            notify_fn(
                delay_seconds=delay_seconds,
                message=(
                    f':bell: Follow-up reminder for {self.incident_id}: '
                    f'{task.value.replace("_", " ").title()} '
                    f'due at {deadline.strftime("%H:%M UTC on %Y-%m-%d")}'
                )
            )

    def summary(self) -> str:
        lines = [f'Follow-up plan for {self.incident_id} (SEV-{self.severity}):']
        for task, deadline in self.task_deadlines().items():
            if deadline:
                lines.append(f'  {task.value}: {deadline.strftime("%Y-%m-%d %H:%M UTC")}')
        return '\n'.join(lines)
Communication Failure Post-Mortems

When the follow-up process fails—a postmortem is published late, a ticket goes unclosed, or an enterprise customer doesn't receive their SLA report—the failure itself should be analyzed in the next incident review. Communication failures are process failures, not personal ones, and they have root causes and fixes just like technical failures.
Postmortem published late.

Causes include: postmortem owner not designated during incident, postmortem template not found, too many concurrent incidents, or review cycle too long. Fix: designate the postmortem owner during the incident and set a calendar deadline before the bridge call closes.
Support tickets unclosed.

Cause: support team not notified of resolution, or bulk-close process not in place. Fix: war room close checklist includes a step to notify the support team lead to bulk-close incident-related tickets.
Enterprise customer received no SLA report.

Cause: no enterprise customer list maintained, TAM contact not in the incident notification flow. Fix: maintain an incident notification list in the CRM and link it to the on-call escalation policy for SEV-1 incidents.
Segmenting Communication by Account Tier

Different customer tiers require different post-incident communication strategies. Enterprise accounts with SLAs expect direct outreach, personalized impact assessments, and dedicated post-incident review calls. SMB accounts typically receive templated email communications with a link to the public postmortem. Regulated customers (HIPAA/PCI/FedRAMP) require documented evidence that notification obligations were met within statutory windows.

The segmentation logic should pull from your CRM to identify affected accounts, classify them by tier and regulatory context, and route notifications through the appropriate channel (automated email, CSM outreach, or executive escalation). Accounts that experienced higher-than-average error rates or latency spikes during the incident window should be flagged for proactive outreach even before they file a support ticket.

# tier_router.py — route post-incident notifications by account tier
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import Enum
from typing import List, Optional
import smtplib
from email.mime.text import MIMEText


class AccountTier(Enum):
    ENTERPRISE = "enterprise"
    GROWTH = "growth"
    SMB = "smb"
    FREE = "free"


class RegulationFlag(Enum):
    HIPAA = "hipaa"
    PCI = "pci"
    FEDRAMP = "fedramp"
    GDPR = "gdpr"
    NONE = "none"


@dataclass
class Account:
    account_id: str
    name: str
    tier: AccountTier
    csm_email: Optional[str]
    regulation: RegulationFlag
    error_rate_increase_pct: float  # relative to baseline during incident


@dataclass
class NotificationResult:
    account_id: str
    channel: str
    status: str
    error: Optional[str] = None


def route_notifications(
    affected_accounts: List[Account],
    incident_id: str,
    postmortem_url: str,
) -> List[NotificationResult]:
    results: List[NotificationResult] = []

    for account in affected_accounts:
        if account.tier == AccountTier.ENTERPRISE or account.error_rate_increase_pct > 25:
            # CSM-led personal outreach
            result = send_csm_outreach(account, incident_id, postmortem_url)
        elif account.regulation != RegulationFlag.NONE:
            # Regulatory notification with compliance evidence
            result = send_regulatory_notification(account, incident_id, postmortem_url)
        elif account.tier in (AccountTier.GROWTH, AccountTier.SMB):
            # Templated email notification
            result = send_template_email(account, incident_id, postmortem_url)
        else:
            # Free tier: postmortem published to public status page only
            result = NotificationResult(
                account_id=account.account_id,
                channel="status_page",
                status="skipped_free_tier",
            )
        results.append(result)

    return results


def send_csm_outreach(account: Account, incident_id: str, postmortem_url: str) -> NotificationResult:
    if not account.csm_email:
        return NotificationResult(account.account_id, "csm", "failed", "no CSM assigned")
    # Draft CSM email stub — real implementation uses CRM API
    body = (
        f"Hi,\n\nFollowing incident {incident_id}, we wanted to reach out personally.\n"
        f"Please find our full postmortem at {postmortem_url}.\n\n"
        f"We would welcome a 30-minute call to walk through impact and prevention steps."
    )
    _send_email(account.csm_email, f"Post-Incident Follow-Up — {incident_id}", body)
    return NotificationResult(account.account_id, "csm_email", "sent")


def send_regulatory_notification(account: Account, incident_id: str, postmortem_url: str) -> NotificationResult:
    # Log notification for compliance audit trail
    audit_record = {
        "incident_id": incident_id,
        "account_id": account.account_id,
        "regulation": account.regulation.value,
        "notified_at": "2026-07-14T10:00:00Z",
        "postmortem_url": postmortem_url,
    }
    _append_audit_log(audit_record)
    return NotificationResult(account.account_id, "regulatory_email", "sent")


def send_template_email(account: Account, incident_id: str, postmortem_url: str) -> NotificationResult:
    body = (
        f"Dear {account.name} team,\n\nOn [date], a service incident ({incident_id}) affected your account.\n"
        f"We have published a full postmortem here: {postmortem_url}.\n\n"
        f"We apologize for the disruption and are committed to preventing recurrence."
    )
    _send_email("support@company.com", f"Service Incident Update — {incident_id}", body)
    return NotificationResult(account.account_id, "template_email", "sent")


def _send_email(to: str, subject: str, body: str) -> None:
    msg = MIMEText(body)
    msg["Subject"] = subject
    msg["From"] = "noreply@company.com"
    msg["To"] = to
    # with smtplib.SMTP("localhost") as s: s.send_message(msg)
    print(f"[email] To={to} Subject={subject}")


def _append_audit_log(record: dict) -> None:
    import json, pathlib
    log_path = pathlib.Path("/var/log/incident-notifications/audit.jsonl")
    log_path.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
    with log_path.open("a") as f:
        f.write(json.dumps(record) + "\n")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    accounts = [
        Account("a1", "Acme Corp", AccountTier.ENTERPRISE, "csm@co.com", RegulationFlag.NONE, 30.5),
        Account("a2", "Startup Inc", AccountTier.SMB, None, RegulationFlag.NONE, 5.0),
        Account("a3", "Hospital Sys", AccountTier.GROWTH, None, RegulationFlag.HIPAA, 12.0),
    ]
    results = route_notifications(accounts, "INC-2026-0042", "https://status.example.com/incidents/42")
    for r in results:
        print(r)
Postmortem Feedback Collection and Iteration

Postmortems should not be published and forgotten. Each public postmortem should include a feedback mechanism — a short survey link or embedded form — so customers can indicate whether the communication was clear, whether remediation commitments were meaningful, and whether they have additional concerns. This feedback closes the loop between the engineering team and the customer base, and over several incidents reveals systemic gaps in how incidents are communicated.

Internally, postmortem review sessions should explicitly assess the quality of customer communications: Was the first status update within the target window? Were escalation paths followed? Did regulated customers receive notifications within statutory deadlines? Capturing these quality signals quarterly enables a data-driven improvement program for incident communications, not just for system reliability.

# postmortem-feedback-survey.yaml
# Embedded in public postmortem page via survey widget

survey:
  id: "post-incident-feedback"
  trigger: "after_postmortem_publish"
  display: "footer_banner"
  questions:
    - id: q1
      text: "Was the initial incident notification timely?"
      type: rating
      scale: 5

    - id: q2
      text: "Were status updates during the incident clear and frequent enough?"
      type: rating
      scale: 5

    - id: q3
      text: "Did this incident significantly impact your operations?"
      type: boolean

    - id: q4
      text: "Are the remediation steps in the postmortem credible and sufficient?"
      type: rating
      scale: 5

    - id: q5
      text: "Additional comments or concerns"
      type: text
      optional: true

  routing:
    low_score_threshold: 3  # average below 3 triggers CSM escalation
    on_low_score:
      action: "create_csm_task"
      crm_tag: "post-incident-low-satisfaction"
    on_submit:
      store: "postgresql://db/postmortem_feedback"
      notify_team: "#incident-comms-feedback"

metrics:
  aggregate_weekly: true
  report_to: "#sre-leadership"
  target_satisfaction: 4.0  # out of 5
Key Takeaways
Resolution is not the end of communication. The follow-up phase—status update, ticket closure, postmortem, SLA credits—is as important as the incident-time communication.
Enterprise customers need proactive SLA credit notification. Don't wait for them to calculate and claim it. Proactive notification converts a reliability failure into a trust opportunity.

Customer-facing postmortems must be in plain language. Internal root cause analysis uses technical detail that customers neither need nor should see. Translate to user-visible impact and actionable prevention.
Follow through on postmortem action items and update the published document. A postmortem with completed action items, marked as such 30 days later, demonstrates operational accountability.
